content:
I'm thinking about how to work in workqueues with the new lifetime model. Let's say we have device private data that looks like this:

#[pin_data]
struct MyGpuPrivateData {
    wq: OwnedWorkqueue<'foo>,
    foo: Foo,
}

and that Workqueue::<'a>::spawn accepts any closure that is 'a. (Note contravariance in 'a.) Then we want to be able to do this:

impl MyGpuPrivateData {
    fn spawn_work(&self) -> Result {
        self.wq.spawn(|| {
            // ok because `wq` borrows from `foo`
            println!("{}", self.foo);
        });
    }
}

But my understanding is that the self-referential pin-init stuff only lets you create such self-referential values when constructing MyGpuPrivateData and not after the fact.

Maybe you can do this by stashing a &Workqueue elsewhere in MyGpuPrivateData?

Thoughts?

cc @Gary Guo @Danilo Krummrich
this isn't self-referential? ah I see it now
shouldn't the transformation &'a Self -> &'foo Foo always be allowed for MyGpuPrivateData?
not sure where the 'foo lifetime would come from, but maybe this can just be a field projection?
There's going to be a with_project API that allows you to this
It uses HRTB to mint 'foo lifetime, and the projected type is going to be &'foo Foo for the foo field

ah cool! is it something like this?

impl MyGpuPrivateData {
    fn with_project<R>(
        &self,
        f: for<'foo> impl FnOnce(MyGpuPrivateDataProjected<'foo>) -> R,
    ) -> R { ... }
}
Yes
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/pin-init/blob/dev/selfref/examples/selfref.rs
I do wonder if and how field projection could make this nice... :thinking:
By the way, I don't know about the lifetimes being field names. For this use-case, you want a lifetime that corresponds to "all fields below wq"

Benno Lossin said:

I do wonder if and how field projection could make this nice... :thinking:

I feel like this needs more than just FP, since we have no way of naming the 'foo lifetime

Alice Ryhl said:

By the way, I don't know about the lifetimes being field names. For this use-case, you want a lifetime that corresponds to "all fields below wq"

I'd imagine we could have some kind of "field groups"

Alice Ryhl said:

By the way, I don't know about the lifetimes being field names. For this use-case, you want a lifetime that corresponds to "all fields below wq"

Just use the first field name that needs to be borrowed. Outlive bounds exist for all borrowed fields based on their drop order.
ah, neat

Benno Lossin said:

I feel like this needs more than just FP, since we have no way of naming the 'foo lifetime

  • if we had a places as lifetimes, we could have self: &Self -> &'{self.foo} Foo, which the borrow checker could permit using in a closure that's spawned on the workqueue.
  • however that signature is only valid if foo is not dropped before the workqueue is finished. currently it seems to me that this is solved by dropping the workqueue first and then foo.

Because of this, std can't improve upon the output lifetime of borrowing unless it also supports self-referential types directly (and then it can check that foo is only ever dropped after wq).
so I don't think that FP will come in handy here

The workqueue could also take a new type Data<'foo> as argument that only contains the references the workqueue actually needs from struct MyGpuPrivateData.

But if this already works, I guess this is more generic as you can always create a new struct within struct MyGpuPrivateData that contains the workqueue, plus the data you want to expose to the workqueue.
@Alice Ryhl I assume we can then relax W: RawWorkItem<ID> + Send + 'static to W: RawWorkItem<ID> + Send + 'wq.
How would you want to handle work items queued on the global system workqueues that must not exceed driver unbind?
Exactly
Such work items would either have to be made 'static or require a different spawn method.
I guess the spawn method would return a handle that cancels work on drop, and it cannot be forgotten?

and it cannot be forgotten?

What do you mean cannot be forgotten? Do you mean mem::forget?
